title: The AIADMK Government's Irresponsible Conduct on the Refugee Issue!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored; camp counts, housing costs, assistance figures, academic years and the twelfth-standard education reference have been checked against the printed pages; and clear OCR character and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: At the Republic Day parade itself, we saw how much respect Ministers, officials and ruling-party members show Chief Minister O. Panneerselvam. Yesterday, *Dina Thanthi* reported on its front page that he had written to the Prime Minister under the headline “Tamil Nadu Government opposes Delhi meeting: Chief Minister writes to Prime Minister Narendra Modi.”

The letter began by saying that the Union Ministry of External Affairs had asked Tamil Nadu to send a senior official to a ministerial-level meeting the next day on the voluntary return of Sri Lankan Tamil refugees. It concluded: “The Tamil Nadu Government considers the proposed ministerial consultation on the voluntary repatriation of Sri Lankan Tamil refugees unnecessary. The meeting should therefore be postponed at this stage.”

Sri Lankan refugees and Tamils displaced by the cruelty of former President Rajapaksa's Government naturally wish to return to their homeland and live in peace. But it remains unknown whether a peaceful life is now possible there or the earlier cruelty will continue. What will happen if they return is unclear.

At such a moment, the Union Government convened an important meeting. Yet the Chief Minister's letter sacrifices the valuable opportunity for a senior Tamil Nadu official to explain the suffering of Sri Lankan Tamils and the work needed for Eelam Tamil welfare after the change of Government in Sri Lanka. Was it not Tamil Nadu's duty to participate and at least insist that the Sri Lankan Government guarantee refugees a good life if they return?

The Chief Minister himself wrote: “I wish to place on record that conditions conducive to Tamil refugees returning to their own country still do not exist in Sri Lanka's Northern and Eastern Provinces.” Could the State not have taken that very position to Delhi, presented it at the meeting and tried to secure a solution?

His letter repeatedly described “the Tamil Nadu Government functioning under the guidance of our revered leader, Revolutionary Leader Amma” and said that “the Tamil Nadu Government following the path of the People's Chief Minister, Revolutionary Leader Amma, has provided accommodation for Sri Lankan Tamil refugees.” It listed benefits supposedly provided under AIADMK rule.

Because Panneerselvam wrote as though benefits were provided only under the AIADMK and nothing had been done by the preceding DMK Government, I am compelled to recall what the DMK Government did for Sri Lankan Tamil refugees.

On 24 October 2009, The Hindu reported under the headline “Facilities at refugee camps good: U.S. Consul-General”: “U.S. Consul-General Andres T. Simkin, on Friday expressed satisfaction with the facilities provided to refugees living in camps in Tamil Nadu.” I wish to remind Panneer of those words now.
Here is a concise list of the benefits provided under the DMK Government.

Sri Lankan Tamils were housed in 115 camps, including two special camps, across twenty-six Tamil Nadu districts. The camps held 73,241 people from 19,340 families. About 31,802 people from 11,288 families lived outside them.

In 2006, the DMK Government repaired the roofs of 9,562 homes at a cost of Rs 2.80 crore and improved basic infrastructure for Rs 3 crore. Toilets and drinking-water facilities were provided in eighty-six camps for Rs 9.12 crore. In 2007-08, new toilets and drinking-water facilities were completed in fifty camps for Rs 5.24 crore. In 2008, Rs 3.12 crore was spent renovating homes and providing toilets and drinking water in seventy-two camps. The 2009-10 Budget allocated Rs 5 crore for such maintenance.

Ninety-six homes were completed at Gangaikondan in Tirunelveli district for Rs 1.20 crore; ninety at Nattarasan Kottai and Chennalakudi for Rs 30.50 lakh; sixty-two at the Gudiyatham camp in Vellore district for Rs 21.08 lakh; sixty-three at Kangeyam in Erode district for Rs 22.68 lakh; and twenty-six at Pavoorchatram in Tirunelveli district for Rs 5.54 lakh. Sri Lankan Tamils were settled in them.

Orders were issued to build 450 homes at Achimangalam in Karur district for Rs 1.16 crore. Work began after approval for 150 new homes at Vedder Colony camp in Coimbatore district for Rs 86 lakh; 115 at Thirumoorthi Nagar for Rs 98 lakh; eighty-seven at Chinnapallikuppam camp in Vellore district for Rs 68.80 lakh; nine at Narayanammalpuram in Tirunelveli district for Rs 7.74 lakh; forty-six at Aladiyur for Rs 39.56 lakh; and sixty at Athiyadipillaiyar Kovil refugee camp for Rs 51.60 lakh.

The Chief Minister's letter also describes how much cash assistance was increased under AIADMK rule. Officials apparently failed to tell Panneerselvam that from 1 August 2006, under the DMK Government, Union and State cash grants were doubled: from Rs 200 to Rs 400 for a family head; Rs 144 to Rs 288 for every adult family member; Rs 90 to Rs 180 for the first child; and Rs 45 to Rs 90 for every other child.

From 2007, the Dr Muthulakshmi Reddy maternity-assistance scheme of Rs 6,000 for pregnant women was extended to Sri Lankan Tamils in camps. Around one thousand people benefited in 2007-08 and 2,242 in 2008-09.

Panneerselvam wrote that his Government made refugee children eligible for free education through the twelfth standard and provided notebooks and textbooks. In truth, during the DMK Government, children of Sri Lankan Tamils in camps already received free education through the twelfth standard, free textbooks, notebooks, uniforms and midday meals, and free bicycles for Plus One students. The beneficiaries numbered 20,106 in 2008-09 and 19,737 in 2009-10.

Orders were issued permitting Sri Lankan students to enter arts colleges in 2008-09 and 2009-10. There were 917 students in 2008-09 and 1,084 in 2009-10. In 2009-10, the Government also ordered that Sri Lankan Tamil students be admitted to self-financing engineering colleges.
Public-health facilities were provided in every camp. From the Chief Minister's Special Relief Fund for Sri Lankan Tamils, Rs 15,000 was provided for accidents and life-saving medical treatment.
Before 2006, electricity was supplied only from 6 p.m. to 6 a.m. After 2006, the DMK Government ordered and provided continuous twenty-four-hour supply.

To improve their livelihoods, camp residents were permitted to leave for work from 6 a.m. to 6 p.m. Those wishing to stay and work in other towns received special permission and needed to return only on the day cash grants were distributed.

At Perarignar Anna's centenary celebration in Kanchipuram on 26 September 2009, the final resolution, read by Deputy Chief Minister younger brother M.K. Stalin, sought a permanent dawn for camp residents. It urged consultation with the Union Government under the Citizenship Act and related laws, so that those who wished could settle permanently in Tamil Nadu, with arrangements for accommodation and livelihoods.
The very next day, 27 September 2009, I wrote to the Prime Minister:

“I request your kind intervention on humanitarian grounds and use of your esteemed offices to resolve this larger issue occupying the centre stage of attention of the Tamil people enabling the permanent settlement of Sri Lankan Tamil Refugees in Tamil Nadu. Once their stay is legally regularised, the Government of Tamil Nadu will be able to take up necessary steps for the betterment of their livelihood, economically and socially.”
Under the DMK Government, a consultative meeting chaired by me resolved that Ministers should personally visit the camps, assess conditions and report to me. Action followed.

To the DMK Government, they were not “Sri Lankan Tamil refugees.” They too were Tamils, joined to us by the umbilical cord. Every effort to sustain them was an effort to sustain ourselves. That emotional conviction guided our work.

Panneerselvam's letter also says his revered Amma passed a resolution in the Tamil Nadu Assembly. That is true! Can we forget Jayalalithaa's resolutions urging the Union Government to have Sri Lanka immediately arrest LTTE leader Prabhakaran and hand him over to India, prevent every member of the banned LTTE from entering India, and, if Sri Lanka could not capture and deport him, send the Indian Army with Sri Lankan permission to help capture him? I think Panneerselvam has indirectly reminded us of those resolutions.

Can we forget Senthooran, transferred under AIADMK rule from the Chengalpattu special camp to the Poonamallee camp, who undertook a continuous hunger strike demanding release of refugees held in special camps until his health severely deteriorated? Can we forget that I called upon the Tamil Nadu Government, on the DMK's behalf, to intervene immediately and save his life?

I strongly condemn the Chief Minister for concealing all this, unnecessarily singing “Amma's” praises in his letter to the Prime Minister, and writing a lame excuse to avoid the Delhi meeting instead of attending, standing face to face and demanding swift relief for Sri Lankan Tamil refugees.
With affection,
M.K.
31 January 2015
